stem: Using the Maclaurin series of \( f(x) = e^{[[a]]x} \), find the \( [[n]] \)th derivative at \( 0 \), \( f^{([[n]])}(0) \).
solution: The Maclaurin series is \( e^{[[a]]x} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{([[a]]x)^{k}}{k!} \), so the coefficient of \( x^{[[n]]} \) is \( \dfrac{[[a]]^{[[n]]}}{[[n]]!} \) and \( f^{([[n]])}(0) = [[n]]!\cdot\dfrac{[[a]]^{[[n]]}}{[[n]]!} = [[a]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the exponent \( [[n]]-1 \) instead of \( [[n]] \).
This uses the exponent \( [[n]]+1 \) instead of \( [[n]] \).
This multiplies base and order instead of exponentiating.
This swaps base and exponent, computing \( [[n]]^{[[a]]} \).
This multiplies by \( [[n]]! \) again; the factorial already cancels.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using the Maclaurin series of \( f(x) = \sin([[a]]x) \), find the \( [[n]] \)th derivative at \( 0 \), \( f^{([[n]])}(0) \).
solution: The Maclaurin series is \( \sin([[a]]x) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (-1)^{k}\dfrac{([[a]]x)^{2k+1}}{(2k+1)!} \); only odd powers appear, and the coefficient of \( x^{[[n]]} \) is \( (-1)^{([[n]]-1)/2}\dfrac{[[a]]^{[[n]]}}{[[n]]!} \). Multiplying by \( [[n]]! \) gives \( f^{([[n]])}(0) = (-1)^{([[n]]-1)/2}[[a]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the alternating sign that comes from repeatedly differentiating sine.
This uses the wrong sign for this particular order.
This uses the exponent \( [[n]]-1 \) instead of \( [[n]] \).
This multiplies by \( [[n]]! \) again; the factorial already cancels.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using the Maclaurin series of \( f(x) = \cos([[a]]x) \), find the \( [[n]] \)th derivative at \( 0 \), \( f^{([[n]])}(0) \).
solution: The Maclaurin series is \( \cos([[a]]x) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (-1)^{k}\dfrac{([[a]]x)^{2k}}{(2k)!} \); only even powers appear, and the coefficient of \( x^{[[n]]} \) is \( (-1)^{[[n]]/2}\dfrac{[[a]]^{[[n]]}}{[[n]]!} \). Multiplying by \( [[n]]! \) gives \( f^{([[n]])}(0) = (-1)^{[[n]]/2}[[a]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the alternating sign that comes from repeatedly differentiating cosine.
This uses the wrong sign for this particular order.
This uses the exponent \( [[n]]-2 \) instead of \( [[n]] \).
This multiplies by \( [[n]]! \) again; the factorial already cancels.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using the Maclaurin series of \( f(x) = \ln(1 + [[a]]x) \), find the \( [[n]] \)th derivative at \( 0 \), \( f^{([[n]])}(0) \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( n! \) instead of \( (n-1)! \); the series coefficient has \( k \), not \( k! \), in the denominator.
This forgets to multiply the series coefficient by \( n! \) at all.
This has the wrong sign for this particular order.
This uses the exponent \( [[n]]-1 \) instead of \( [[n]] \) on \( [[a]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: What is the coefficient of \( x^{[[n]]} \) in the Maclaurin series of \( f(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 - [[a]]x} \)?
solution: The geometric series gives \( \dfrac{1}{1-[[a]]x} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} ([[a]]x)^{k} \), so the coefficient of \( x^{[[n]]} \) is \( [[a]]^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the exponent \( [[n]]-1 \) instead of \( [[n]] \).
This uses the exponent \( [[n]]+1 \) instead of \( [[n]] \).
This divides by \( [[n]]! \), which is only needed when converting a derivative to a coefficient, not here.
This swaps base and exponent, computing \( [[n]]^{[[a]]} \).
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
